From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Babylonian_tablet_recording_Halley%27s_comet.jpg/330px-Babylonian_tablet_recording_Halley%27s_comet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Babylonian_tablet_recording_Halley%27s_comet.jpg)

A [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") tablet recording [Halley's Comet](/wiki/Halley%27s_Comet "Halley's Comet") in 164 BC

For the cosmology of ancient Babylon, see [Ancient Near Eastern cosmology](/wiki/Ancient_Near_Eastern_cosmology "Ancient Near Eastern cosmology").

**Babylonian astronomy** was the study or recording of [celestial objects](/wiki/Celestial_object "Celestial object") during the early history of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"). The [numeral system](/wiki/Numeral_system "Numeral system") used, [sexagesimal](/wiki/Sexagesimal "Sexagesimal"), was based on 60, as opposed to ten in the modern [decimal system](/wiki/Decimal "Decimal"). This system simplified the calculating and recording of unusually great and small numbers.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriberg2019-1)

During the 8th and 7th centuries BC, Babylonian astronomers developed a new [empirical](/wiki/Empirical "Empirical") approach to astronomy. They began studying and recording their [belief](/wiki/Belief "Belief") system and philosophies dealing with an ideal nature of the [universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe") and began employing an [internal logic](/wiki/Consistency_proof "Consistency proof") within their predictive planetary systems. This was an important contribution to astronomy and the [philosophy of science](/wiki/Philosophy_of_science "Philosophy of science"), and some modern scholars have thus referred to this approach as a scientific revolution.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown20005–6-2) This approach to astronomy was adopted and further developed in [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_astronomy "Ancient Greek astronomy") and [Hellenistic astrology](/wiki/Hellenistic_astrology "Hellenistic astrology"). Classical Greek and Latin sources frequently use the term [Chaldeans](/wiki/Chaldea "Chaldea") for the philosophers, who were considered as [priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest")-[scribes](/wiki/Scribes "Scribes") specializing in astronomical and other forms of [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination"). Babylonian astronomy paved the way for modern astrology and is responsible for its spread across the Graeco-Roman empire during the 2nd-century [Hellenistic Period](/wiki/Hellenistic_Period "Hellenistic Period"). The Babylonians used the [sexagesimal](/wiki/Sexagesimal "Sexagesimal") system to trace the planets' transits, by dividing the 360 degree sky into 30 degrees, they assigned 12 zodiacal signs to the stars along the ecliptic.

Only fragments of Babylonian astronomy have survived, consisting largely of contemporary clay tablets containing [astronomical diaries](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomical_diaries "Babylonian astronomical diaries"), [ephemerides](/wiki/Ephemerides "Ephemerides") and procedure texts, hence current knowledge of Babylonian planetary theory is in a fragmentary state.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaboe1958209-3) Nevertheless, the surviving fragments show that Babylonian astronomy was the first "successful attempt at giving a refined mathematical description of astronomical phenomena" and that "all subsequent varieties of scientific astronomy, in the [Hellenistic world](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_astronomy#Hellenistic_astronomy "Ancient Greek astronomy"), in [India](/wiki/Indian_astronomy "Indian astronomy"), in [Islam](/wiki/Astronomy_in_the_medieval_Islamic_world "Astronomy in the medieval Islamic world"), and in the West ... depend upon Babylonian astronomy in decisive and fundamental ways".[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaboe197421-4)

## Old Babylonian astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Old Babylonian astronomy")]

See also: [Babylonian star catalogues](/wiki/Babylonian_star_catalogues "Babylonian star catalogues")

An object labelled the ivory prism was recovered from the ruins of [Nineveh](/wiki/Nineveh "Nineveh"). First presumed to be describing rules to a game, its use was later deciphered to be a unit converter for calculating the movement of [celestial bodies](/wiki/Celestial_bodies "Celestial bodies") and [constellations](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1951-5)

Babylonian astronomers developed zodiacal signs. They are made up of the division of the sky into three sets of thirty degrees and the constellations that inhabit each sector.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERochberg-Halton1983-6)

The [MUL.APIN](/wiki/MUL.APIN "MUL.APIN") contains [catalogues of stars and constellations](/wiki/Babylonian_star_catalogues "Babylonian star catalogues") as well as schemes for predicting [heliacal risings](/wiki/Heliacal_rising "Heliacal rising") and settings of the planets, and lengths of daylight as measured by a [water clock](/wiki/Water_clock "Water clock"), [gnomon](/wiki/Gnomon "Gnomon"), shadows, and [intercalations](/wiki/Intercalation_(timekeeping) "Intercalation (timekeeping)"). The Babylonian GU text arranges stars in 'strings' that lie along declination circles and thus measure right-ascensions or time intervals, and also employs the stars of the zenith, which are also separated by given right-ascensional differences.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPingree1998-7)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERochberg2004-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans1998296–297-9)

### Planetary theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Planetary theory")]

The Babylonians were the first civilization known to possess a functional theory of the planets.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans1998296–297-9) The oldest surviving planetary astronomical text is the Babylonian [Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa](/wiki/Venus_tablet_of_Ammisaduqa "Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa"), a 7th-century BC copy of a list of observations of the motions of the planet Venus that probably dates as early as the second millennium BC. The [Babylonian astrologers](/wiki/Babylonian_astrology "Babylonian astrology") also laid the foundations of what would eventually become [Western astrology](/wiki/Western_astrology "Western astrology").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolden20061-10) The *[Enuma anu enlil](/wiki/Enuma_anu_enlil "Enuma anu enlil")*, written during the [Neo-Assyrian](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian "Neo-Assyrian") period in the 7th century BC,[[11]](#cite_note-11) comprises a list of [omens](/wiki/Omen "Omen") and their relationships with various celestial phenomena including the motions of the planets.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambert198793-12)

### Cosmology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Cosmology")]

Main article: [Ancient near eastern cosmology](/wiki/Ancient_near_eastern_cosmology "Ancient near eastern cosmology")

In contrast to the [world view](/wiki/World_view "World view") presented in Mesopotamian and [Assyro-Babylonian literature](/wiki/Assyro-Babylonian_literature "Assyro-Babylonian literature"), particularly [in Mesopotamian](/wiki/Mesopotamian_mythology "Mesopotamian mythology") and [Babylonian mythology](/wiki/Babylonian_mythology "Babylonian mythology"), very little is known about the [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology") and world view of the ancient Babylonian astrologers and astronomers.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERochberg-Halton1983-6) This is largely due to the current fragmentary state of Babylonian planetary theory,[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaboe1958-13) and also due to Babylonian astronomy and cosmology largely being separate endeavors. Nevertheless, traces of cosmology can be found in Babylonian literature and mythology.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERochberg2002679-14)

### Omens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Omens")]

It was a common Mesopotamian belief that [gods](/wiki/Gods "Gods") could and did indicate future events to mankind through omens; sometimes through animal entrails, but most often they believed omens could be read through [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") and [astrology](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology"). Since omens via the planets were produced without any human action, they were seen as more powerful. But they believed the events these omens foretold were also avoidable. The relationship Mesopotamians had with omens can be seen in the Omen Compendia, a Babylonian text composed starting from the beginning of the second millennium on-wards.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHungerPingree1999-15) It is the primary source text that tells us that ancient Mesopotamians saw omens as preventable. The text also contains information on [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") rites to avert evil, or “nam-bur-bi”, a term later adopted by the [Akkadians](/wiki/Akkadians "Akkadians") as “namburbu”, meaning roughly, “[the evil] loosening”. The god Ea was the one believed to send the omens. Concerning the severity of omens, eclipses were seen as the most dangerous.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHungerPingree19991–33-16)

The [Enuma Anu Enlil](/wiki/Enuma_Anu_Enlil "Enuma Anu Enlil") is a series of cuneiform tablets that gives insight on different sky omens Babylonian astronomers observed.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHungerPingree199912–20-17) Celestial bodies such as the Sun and Moon were given significant power as omens. Reports from [Nineveh](/wiki/Nineveh "Nineveh") and [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"), circa 2500-670 B.C., show lunar omens observed by the Mesopotamians. "When the moon disappears, evil will befall the land. When the moon disappears out of its reckoning, an eclipse will take place".[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Astrolabes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Astrolabes")]

The astrolabes (not to be mistaken for the later [astronomical measurement device](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe") of the same name) are one of the earliest documented [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") tablets that discuss [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") and date back to the Old Babylonian Kingdom. They are a list of thirty-six stars connected with the months in a year,[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERochberg-Halton1983-6) generally considered to be written between 1800 and 1100 B.C. No complete texts have been found, but there is a modern compilation by [Theophilus Pinches](/wiki/Theophilus_Pinches "Theophilus Pinches"), assembled from texts housed in the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") that is considered excellent by other historians who specialize in Babylonian astronomy. Two other texts concerning the astrolabes that should be mentioned are the Brussels and Berlin compilations. They offer similar information to the [Pinches anthology](/wiki/Chronicle_P "Chronicle P"), but do contain some differing information from each other.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1949-19)

The thirty-six stars that make up the astrolabes are believed to be derived from the astronomical traditions from three Mesopotamian city-states, [Elam](/wiki/Elam "Elam"), [Akkad](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire"), and [Amurru](/wiki/Amurru_kingdom "Amurru kingdom"). The stars followed and possibly charted by these city-states are identical stars to the ones in the astrolabes. Each region had a set of twelve stars it followed, which combined equals the thirty-six stars in the astrolabes. The twelve stars of each region also correspond to the months of the year. The two cuneiform texts that provide the information for this claim are the large star list “K 250” and “K 8067”. Both of these tablets were translated and transcribed by Weidner. During the reign of [Hammurabi](/wiki/Hammurabi "Hammurabi") these three separate traditions were combined. This combining also ushered in a more scientific approach to astronomy as connections to the original three traditions weakened. The increased use of science in astronomy is evidenced by the traditions from these three regions being arranged in accordance to the paths of the stars of [Ea](/wiki/Ea_(Babylonian_god) "Ea (Babylonian god)"), [Anu](/wiki/Anu "Anu"), and [Enlil](/wiki/Enlil "Enlil"), an astronomical system contained and discussed in the MUL.APIN.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1949-19)

### MUL.APIN

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: MUL.APIN")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/MulApin-BritishMuseum.jpg/250px-MulApin-BritishMuseum.jpg)](/wiki/File:MulApin-BritishMuseum.jpg)

Mul.apin cuneiform tablet

Main article: [MUL.APIN](/wiki/MUL.APIN "MUL.APIN")

MUL.APIN is a collection of two cuneiform tablets (Tablet 1 and Tablet 2) that document aspects of Babylonian astronomy such as the movement of [celestial bodies](/wiki/Celestial_bodies "Celestial bodies") and records of [solstices](/wiki/Solstices "Solstices") and [eclipses](/wiki/Eclipses "Eclipses").[[20]](#cite_note-BA3-20) Each tablet is also split into smaller sections called Lists. It was comprised in the general time frame of the astrolabes and [Enuma Anu Enlil](/wiki/Enuma_Anu_Enlil "Enuma Anu Enlil"), evidenced by similar themes, mathematical principles, and occurrences.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHungerPingree199957–65-21)

Tablet 1 houses information that closely parallels information contained in astrolabe B. The similarities between Tablet 1 and astrolabe B show that the authors were inspired by the same source for at least some of the information. There are six lists of stars on this tablet that relate to sixty constellations in charted paths of the three groups of Babylonian star paths, Ea, Anu, and Enlil. There are also additions to the paths of both Anu and Enlil that are not found in astrolabe B.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHungerPingree199957–65-21)

### Relationship of calendar, mathematics and astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Relationship of calendar, mathematics and astronomy")]

The exploration of the Sun, Moon, and other celestial bodies affected the development of Mesopotamian culture. The study of the sky led to the development of a calendar and advanced mathematics in these societies. The Babylonians were not the first complex society to develop a calendar globally and nearby in North Africa, the Egyptians developed a calendar of their own. The Egyptian calendar was solar based, while the Babylonian calendar was lunar based. A potential blend between the two that has been noted by some historians is the adoption of a crude leap year by the Babylonians after the Egyptians developed one. The Babylonian leap year shares no similarities with the leap year practiced today. It involved the addition of a thirteenth month as a means to re-calibrate the calendar to better match the growing season.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlmstead1938-22)

Babylonian priests were the ones responsible for developing new forms of mathematics and did so to better calculate the movements of celestial bodies. One such priest, [Naburimannu](/wiki/Naburimannu "Naburimannu"), is the first documented Babylonian astronomer. He was a priest for the moon god and is credited with writing lunar and eclipse computation tables as well as other elaborate mathematical calculations. The computation tables are organized in seventeen or eighteen tables that document the orbiting speeds of planets and the Moon. His work was later recounted by astronomers during the Seleucid dynasty.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlmstead1938-22)

### Aurorae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Aurorae")]

A team of scientists at the [University of Tsukuba](/wiki/University_of_Tsukuba "University of Tsukuba") studied Assyrian cuneiform tablets, reporting unusual red skies which might be [aurorae](/wiki/Aurora "Aurora") incidents, caused by [geomagnetic storms](/wiki/Geomagnetic_storm "Geomagnetic storm") between 680 and 650 BC.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayakawaMitsumaEbiharaMiyake2019-23)

## Neo-Babylonian astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Neo-Babylonian astronomy")]

Neo-Babylonian astronomy refers to the astronomy developed by [Chaldean](/wiki/Chaldea "Chaldea") astronomers during the [Neo-Babylonian](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian_Empire "Neo-Babylonian Empire"), [Achaemenid](/wiki/Achaemenid_Assyria "Achaemenid Assyria"), [Seleucid](/wiki/Seleucid_Empire "Seleucid Empire"), and [Parthian](/wiki/Parthia "Parthia") periods of Mesopotamian history. The systematic records in [Babylonian astronomical diaries](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomical_diaries "Babylonian astronomical diaries") allowed for the observation of a repeating 18-year [Saros cycle](/wiki/Saros_cycle "Saros cycle") of lunar eclipses.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaboeBrittonHendersonNeugebauer1991-24)

### Arithmetical and geometrical methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Arithmetical and geometrical methods")]

Though there is a lack of surviving material on Babylonian planetary theory,[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaboe1958-13) it appears most of the Chaldean astronomers were concerned mainly with [ephemerides](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris") and not with theory. It had been thought that most of the predictive Babylonian planetary models that have survived were usually strictly [empirical](/wiki/Empirical "Empirical") and [arithmetical](/wiki/Arithmetic "Arithmetic"), and usually did not involve [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry"), [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology"), or speculative [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") like that of the later [Hellenistic models](/wiki/Hellenistic_astronomy "Hellenistic astronomy"),[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESarton1955-25) though the Babylonian astronomers were concerned with the philosophy dealing with the ideal nature of the early [universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2000-26) Babylonian procedure texts describe, and ephemerides employ, arithmetical procedures to compute the time and place of significant astronomical events.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaboe200140–62-27) More recent analysis of previously unpublished [cuneiform tablets](/wiki/Cuneiform_script "Cuneiform script") in the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum"), dated between 350 and 50 BC, demonstrates that Babylonian astronomers sometimes used geometrical methods, prefiguring the methods of the [Oxford Calculators](/wiki/Oxford_Calculators "Oxford Calculators"), to describe the motion of [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") over time in an abstract mathematical space.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOssendrijver2016-28)

Aside from occasional interactions between the two, Babylonian astronomy was largely independent from [Babylonian cosmology](/wiki/Ancient_near_eastern_cosmology "Ancient near eastern cosmology").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERochberg2002679-14) Whereas Greek astronomers expressed "prejudice in favor of circles or spheres rotating with uniform motion", such a preference did not exist for Babylonian astronomers.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPingree1992557-29)

Contributions made by the Chaldean astronomers during this period include the discovery of [eclipse cycles](/wiki/Eclipse_cycle "Eclipse cycle") and [saros cycles](/wiki/Saros_cycle "Saros cycle"), and many accurate astronomical observations. For example, they observed that the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun")'s motion along the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic") was not uniform, though they were unaware of why this was; it is today known that this is due to the Earth moving in an [elliptic orbit](/wiki/Elliptic_orbit "Elliptic orbit") around the Sun, with the Earth moving swifter when it is nearer to the Sun at [perihelion](/wiki/Perihelion "Perihelion") and moving slower when it is farther away at [aphelion](/wiki/Aphelion "Aphelion").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeverington20036–7-30)

### Heliocentric astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Heliocentric astronomy")]

Main article: [Seleucus of Seleucia](/wiki/Seleucus_of_Seleucia "Seleucus of Seleucia")

The only surviving planetary model from among the Chaldean astronomers is that of the Hellenistic [Seleucus of Seleucia](/wiki/Seleucus_of_Seleucia "Seleucus of Seleucia") (b. 190 BC), who supported the Greek [Aristarchus of Samos](/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos "Aristarchus of Samos")' [heliocentric](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") model.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESarton1955169-31) Seleucus is known from the writings of [Plutarch](/wiki/Plutarch "Plutarch"), [Aetius](/wiki/Aetius_(philosopher) "Aetius (philosopher)"), [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), and [Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Zakariya_al-Razi "Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi"). The Greek geographer Strabo lists Seleucus as one of the four most influential astronomers, who came from Hellenistic Seleuceia on the Tigris, alongside [Kidenas](/wiki/Kidenas "Kidenas") (Kidinnu), [Naburianos](/wiki/Naburimannu "Naburimannu") (Naburimannu), and [Sudines](/wiki/Sudines "Sudines"). Their works were originally written in the [Akkadian language](/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") and later translated into [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1987-32) Seleucus, however, was unique among them in that he was the only one known to have supported the heliocentric theory of planetary motion proposed by Aristarchus,[[33]](#cite_note-33) where the [Earth rotated](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") around its own axis which in turn revolved around the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"). According to Plutarch, Seleucus even proved the heliocentric system through [reasoning](/wiki/Reasoning "Reasoning"), though it is not known what arguments he used.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1987-32)

According to [Lucio Russo](/wiki/Lucio_Russo "Lucio Russo"), his arguments were probably related to the phenomenon of [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide").[[34]](#cite_note-34) Seleucus correctly theorized that tides were caused by the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), although he believed that the interaction was mediated by the [Earth's atmosphere](/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere "Earth's atmosphere"). He noted that the tides varied in time and strength in different parts of the world. According to [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") (1.1.9), Seleucus was the first to state that the [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide") are due to the attraction of the Moon, and that the height of the tides depends on the Moon's position relative to the Sun.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1987527-35)

According to [Bartel Leendert van der Waerden](/wiki/Bartel_Leendert_van_der_Waerden "Bartel Leendert van der Waerden"), Seleucus may have proved the heliocentric theory by determining the constants of a [geometric](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry") model for the heliocentric theory and by developing methods to compute planetary positions using this model. He may have used [trigonometric](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry") methods that were available in his time, as he was a contemporary of [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1987-32)

None of his original writings or Greek translations have survived, though a fragment of his work has survived only in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") translation, which was later referred to by the [Persian philosopher](/wiki/Iranian_philosophy "Iranian philosophy") [Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Zakariya_al-Razi "Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi") (865-925).[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Babylonian influence on Hellenistic astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Babylonian influence on Hellenistic astronomy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Babylonian_astronomy "Special:EditPage/Babylonian astronomy") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(November 2012)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Many of the works of ancient [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_civilization "Hellenistic civilization") writers (including [mathematicians](/wiki/Greek_mathematics "Greek mathematics"), [astronomers](/wiki/Greek_astronomy "Greek astronomy"), and [geographers](/wiki/Geographers "Geographers")) have been preserved up to the present time, or some aspects of their work and thought are still known through later references. However, achievements in these fields by earlier [ancient Near Eastern](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East") civilizations, notably those in [Babylonia](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia"), were forgotten for a long time. Since the discovery of key archaeological sites in the 19th century, many [cuneiform](/wiki/Cuneiform_script "Cuneiform script") writings on [clay tablets](/wiki/Clay_tablet "Clay tablet") have been found, some of them related to [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"). Most known astronomical tablets have been described by [Abraham Sachs](/wiki/Abraham_Sachs "Abraham Sachs") and later published by [Otto Neugebauer](/wiki/Otto_Neugebauer "Otto Neugebauer") in the *Astronomical Cuneiform Texts* (*ACT*). Herodotus writes that the Greeks learned such aspects of astronomy as the [gnomon](/wiki/Gnomon "Gnomon") and the idea of the day being split into two halves of twelve from the Babylonians.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1949-19) Other sources point to Greek pardegms, a stone with 365-366 holes carved into it to represent the days in a year, from the Babylonians as well.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1951-5)

### Influence on Hipparchus and Ptolemy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Influence on Hipparchus and Ptolemy")]

In 1900, Franz Xaver Kugler demonstrated that Ptolemy had stated in his *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* IV.2 that Hipparchus improved the values for the Moon's periods known to him from "even more ancient astronomers" by comparing eclipse observations made earlier by "the Chaldeans", and by himself. However Kugler found that the periods that Ptolemy attributes to Hipparchus had already been used in Babylonian [ephemerides](/wiki/Ephemerides "Ephemerides"), specifically the collection of texts nowadays called "**System B**" (sometimes attributed to [Kidinnu](/wiki/Kidinnu "Kidinnu")). Apparently Hipparchus only confirmed the validity of the periods he learned from the Chaldeans by his newer observations. Later Greek knowledge of this specific Babylonian theory is confirmed by 2nd-century [papyrus](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus"), which contains 32 lines of a single column of calculations for the Moon using this same "System B", but written in Greek on papyrus rather than in cuneiform on clay tablets.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAaboe200162–65-37)

### Means of transmission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylonian_astronomy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Means of transmission")]

Historians have found evidence that Athens during the late 5th century may have been aware of Babylonian astronomy, astronomers, or astronomical concepts and practices through the documentation by Xenophon of Socrates telling his students to study astronomy to the extent of being able to tell the time of night from the stars. This skill is referenced in the poem of [Aratus](/wiki/Aratus "Aratus"), which discusses telling the time of night from the zodiacal signs.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_der_Waerden1951-5)
